title: Sorption and diffusion of methanol and water in PVDF-g-PSSA and Nafion� 117 polymer electrolyte membranes
abstract:
Sorption and Diffusion of Methanol and Water in PVDF-g-
PSSA and NafionT 117 Polymer Electrolyte Membranes
SAMI HIETALA, SIRKKA LIISA MAUNU, FRANCISKA SUNDHOLM
Laboratory of Polymer Chemistry, University of Helsinki, P.O. Box 55, 00014 HY Finland
Received 2 February 2000; revised 11 September 2000; accepted 13 September 2000
ABSTRACT: Sorption and diffusion properties of poly(vinylidene ﬂuoride)-graft-poly-
(styrene sulfonic acid) (PVDF-g-PSSA) and Naﬁont 117 polymer electrolyte membranes
were studied in water/methanol mixtures. The two types of membranes were found to
have different sorption properties. The Naﬁon 117 membrane was found to have a
maximum in-solvent uptake around 0.4 to 0.6 mole fraction of methanol, while the
PVDF-g-PSSA membranes took up less solvent with increasing methanol concentra-
tion. The proton NMR spectra were recorded for membranes immersed in deuterated
water/methanol mixtures. The spectra showed that the hydroxyl protons inside the
membrane exhibit resonance lines different from the resonance lines of hydroxyl
protons in the external solvent. The spectral features of the lines of these internal
hydroxyl groups in the membranes were different in the Naﬁon membrane compared
with the PVDF-g-PSSA membranes. Diffusion measurements with the pulsed ﬁeld
gradient NMR (PFG-NMR) method showed that the diffusion coefﬁcient of the internal
hydroxyl groups in the solvent immersed Naﬁon membrane mirrors the changes in the
diffusion coefﬁcients of hydroxyl and methyl protons in the external solvent. For the
PVDF-g-PSSA membranes, a decrease in the diffusion coefﬁcient of the internal hy-
droxyl protons was seen with increasing methanol concentration. These results indicate
that the morphology and chemical structure of the membranes have an effect on their
solvent sorption and diffusion characteristics. © 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci
B: Polym Phys 38: 3277–3284, 2000
Keywords:
polymer electrolyte membranes; fuel cells; methanol permeability; sol-
vent uptake; self diffusion; NMR spectroscopy
INTRODUCTION
Membranes with high proton conductivity are po-
tentially useful as electrolytes and separators in
electrochemical cells, including fuel cells. The
state-of-the-art membranes used in these applica-
tions are based on the perﬂuorinated membranes,
such as the Naﬁont series from DuPont, and the
Aciplext series from Asahi Chemical. These mem-
branes show good chemical resistance and rea-
sonable performance. However, due to a complex
synthesis route, including the ﬂuorination of the
starting materials, it is expensive to manufacture
these membrane types.1
Poly(vinylidene ﬂuoride)-graft-poly(styrene sul-
fonic acid) (PVDF-g-PSSA) polymer electrolyte
membranes are materials made for electrochemical
applications, such as fuel cells. The preparation, as
well as the chemical and electrochemical character-
ization of the PVDF-g-PSSA membranes, have been
reported in detail in references 2–5 and references
cited therein. The membrane preparation is based
on the well-known radiation grafting method.6–10
The
starting
material,
commercially
available
PVDF foil, is subjected to electron irradiation, fol-
lowed by immersion into a grafting solution con-
taining a styrene monomer. The amount of the
grafted polystyrene can be controlled by careful
Correspondence
to:

Hietala
(E-mail:
sami.hietala@
helsinki.ﬁ)
Journal of Polymer Science: Part B: Polymer Physics, Vol. 38, 3277–3284 (2000)
© 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
3277
 choice of the grafting conditions. After the grafting
the material is sulfonated with chlorosulfonic acid,
which reacts with the aromatic rings of polystyrene
and results in the formation of PVDF-g-PSSA mem-
branes, which has the tentative chemical structure
shown in Figure 1. Due to the ﬂexibility of the
preparation technique, membrane characteristics
such as ion exchange capacity, water content, and
proton conductivity of the membranes can be con-
trolled. As only a few preparation steps are required
and the processing of the polymer into a membrane
is circumvented, the irradiation grafting methods
offer advantages in terms of economic feasibility,
and remain of great interest as a route to new, less
expensive polymer electrolyte membranes.
The concept of the direct methanol fuel cell
(DMFC) has raised interest, especially for trans-
port applications during recent years. The inter-
est has been mainly due to the development of
improved electrocatalysts for methanol oxidation
and the ease of usage of the liquid fuel compared
with hydrogen. One of the main problems in-
volved with the technology is the methanol cross-
over from anode to cathode.11 This problem arises
from the high methanol permeability through the
perﬂuorosulfonate membranes and leads to unde-
sired electrode reactions and lower overall efﬁ-
ciency. In order to improve the feasibility of the
DMFC application, the membranes should have
improved methanol barrier properties.
The barrier properties of different polymer
electrolyte membranes for different gases have
been widely studied,12–16 mostly with regard to
the membrane hydrogen and oxygen permeability
for application in a hydrogen fuel cell. In general,
the barrier properties of different membrane
types for a hydrogen fuel cell have been satisfac-
tory despite a minor concern for the side reactions
on the electrodes due to permeated fuel or oxygen.
For hydrogen permeation through the mem-
branes it was concluded that the gas permeates
mainly through the water phase in the mem-
branes, and that the permeation rate increases
with
increasing
water
content
of
the
mem-
brane.12,13,16 We showed that the hydrogen gas
permeation properties of PVDF-g-PSSA mem-
branes and Naﬁon 117 differ.16 The Naﬁon 117
membrane that contained a low amount of water
had a higher hydrogen permeability coefﬁcient in
the wet state than the corresponding PVDF-g-
PSSA membranes. This observation was ex-
plained by differences in the chemical structure
and morphology of the membranes, which con-
tribute to the gas permeation characteristics. The
observed differences in gas permeation character-
istics motivated us to look at whether the meth-
anol barrier properties were similarly affected.
The permeation processes through membranes
are governed by the solubility and diffusion of the
permeating solvent in the membrane at the given
temperature and conditions. The solubility of the
solvent in the polymeric membrane is dependent
on the chemical nature of the solvent and of the
corresponding membrane, while the diffusion is
determined largely by the morphology of the
membrane17 and the properties of the solvent.
The resulting permeation rate is then determined
by a complex interplay between the properties of
the system, including the morphology of the poly-
mer, restrictions on the polymer swellability by
crosslinks, and the chemical properties of the sol-
vent and polymer, such as hydrophilicity or hy-
drophobicity.
The perﬂuorosulfonated membranes are not
chemically crosslinked and are known to be solu-
ble in alcohol solutions, and Naﬁon 117 was
shown to dissolve gradually in water/methanol
mixtures
in
a
repetitive
series
of
measure-
ments.18 For Naﬁon 117, it has been shown that
the solvent uptake per sulfonic acid group in Na-
ﬁon is the same for membranes equilibrated in
pure water or pure methanol.18 However, in wa-
ter/methanol mixtures, Naﬁon 117 exhibits in-
creased solvent uptake in the concentration range
0.4 to 0.6 mole fractions of methanol.
While the sorption data give a qualitative mea-
sure of the solubility of the solvent into the poly-
mer, it does not give information about the diffu-
sion of the solvent in the polymer. The pulsed ﬁeld
gradient NMR method (PFG-NMR)19 gives access
to the diffusion coefﬁcients of mobile molecules,
provided the nuclei in question have individual
chemical shifts and sufﬁciently long relaxation
times. By employing PFG-NMR to measure the
diffusion of solvent in the membrane, the solvent
permeation behavior in the membranes can be
Figure 1.
Tentative chemical structure of the PVDF-
g-PSSA polymer electrolytes.
